content:
# John Chapter 1 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 1 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 1:1 — "Word order and naming of the Logos" for Greek λόγος / θεὸς ἦν ὁ λόγος

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "In [the] beginning was the Logos, and the Logos was with the God, and God was the Logos." | Preserves Greek word order and retains "Logos" untranslated; retains articles to show anarthrous θεός |
| LOGOS | "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God." | Standard English word order; translates λόγος as "Word" |
| PNEUMA | "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God." | Same as LOGOS; standard English rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA rendering — "the Word was God" in conventional English word order. EMET's literal Greek word order ("God was the Logos") is grammatically faithful but risks misreading in English (modalism). The note on the anarthrous θεός is preserved in EMET's textual apparatus.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's retention of "Logos" and Greek word order noted as significant but not adopted for the consensus text.

---

## 1:2 — "Pronomial reference" for Greek οὗτος

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "This [one] was in [the] beginning with the God." | Literal rendering of οὗτος as "this one"; retains article on θεός |
| LOGOS | "He was in the beginning with God." | Smooth English pronoun substitution |
| PNEUMA | "He was with God in the beginning." | Dynamic reordering — "with God" precedes "in the beginning" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "He was in the beginning with God" — preserving the word order while using a natural English pronoun.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's reordering is acceptable but departs from the Greek clause structure without cause.

---

## 1:3 — "All things were made" vs. "came into being" for Greek ἐγένετο

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "All things through him came into being, and apart from him came into being not even one [thing] that has come into being." | Literal; preserves threefold repetition of γίνομαι |
| LOGOS | "All things were made through him, and without him was not any thing made that was made." | Formal; uses "made" for γίνομαι |
| PNEUMA | "Everything came into existence through him, and nothing that exists was made without him." | Dynamic; condenses the repetition |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "Made" is clear and dignified. PNEUMA condenses the triple use of γίνομαι, losing the emphatic repetition of the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's literalism noted for scholarly reference.

---

## 1:4 — "light of men" vs. "light of the human race" for Greek τὸ φῶς τῶν ἀνθρώπων

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the life was the light of the men" | Literal with article |
| LOGOS | "the life was the light of men" | Standard formal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "that life was the light of the human race" | Gender-inclusive expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "the light of men" — as the conventional rendering. PNEUMA's "human race" is interpretively valid but adds specificity not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:5 — "has not overcome" vs. "never extinguished" for Greek κατέλαβεν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the darkness did not overcome it" | Past tense, "overcome" |
| LOGOS | "the darkness has not overcome it" | Present perfect, "overcome" |
| PNEUMA | "the darkness has never extinguished it" | Present perfect, "extinguished" — more vivid image |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "has not overcome it." The verb κατέλαβεν carries a dual sense (overcome/comprehend), and "overcome" preserves both possibilities better than "extinguished."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "extinguished" narrows the semantic range.

---

## 1:6 — Sentence structure and naming for Greek Ἰωάννης

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "There came a man, having been sent from God, [the] name of him [was] Ioannes." | Literal Greek structure; uses transliterated "Ioannes" |
| LOGOS | "There was a man sent from God, whose name was John." | Formal English with anglicized "John" |
| PNEUMA | "God sent a man named John." | Compressed dynamic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's compression loses the stately pace of the Prologue. EMET's transliteration is scholarly but not necessary for a consensus text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:7 — "as a witness, to bear witness" vs. "to testify" for Greek μαρτυρίαν / μαρτυρήσῃ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "This [one] came for testimony, that he might testify concerning the light, that all might believe through him." | Literal; "testimony"/"testify" |
| LOGOS | "He came as a witness, to bear witness about the light, that all might believe through him." | Formal; witness/bear witness |
| PNEUMA | "He came as a witness to testify about the light, so that through him everyone might believe." | Dynamic; reorders final clause |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "as a witness, to bear witness" — preserving the cognate relationship in the Greek (μαρτυρία/μαρτυρέω).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:8 — "came to bear witness" vs. "came only to point to the light" for Greek μαρτυρήσῃ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "That [one] was not the light, but [came] that he might testify concerning the light." | Literal |
| LOGOS | "He was not the light, but came to bear witness about the light." | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "He himself was not the light — he came only to point to the light." | Dynamic; interprets "testify" as "point to"; adds "only" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "point to" is interpretive and "only" adds emphasis not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:9 — "gives light to everyone" vs. "enlightens every man" for Greek φωτίζει πάντα ἄνθρωπον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The true light, which enlightens every man, was coming into the world." | Literal; "enlightens every man" |
| LOGOS | "The true light, which gives light to everyone, was coming into the world." | Formal; gender-neutral "everyone" |
| PNEUMA | "The true light that gives light to every person was coming into the world." | Dynamic; "every person" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "gives light to everyone." Minor variation; all three are acceptable.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minimal divergence.

---

## 1:10 — "did not know him" vs. "didn't recognize him" for Greek οὐκ ἔγνω

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the world did not know him" | Literal "know" |
| LOGOS | "yet the world did not know him" | Formal "know" with adversative "yet" |
| PNEUMA | "yet the world didn't recognize him" | Dynamic; "recognize" instead of "know" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "did not know him." PNEUMA's "recognize" is valid but narrows γινώσκω from broad knowing to visual recognition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:11 — "did not receive him" vs. "rejected him" for Greek οὐ παρέλαβον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "To the own [things] he came, and the own [people] did not receive him." | Literal; distinguishes neuter/masculine in "own" |
| LOGOS | "He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him." | Formal; clear English |
| PNEUMA | "He came to his own people, and his own people rejected him." | Dynamic; "rejected" is stronger than "did not receive" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "did not receive him." PNEUMA's "rejected" adds active hostility beyond what παρέλαβον implies; the Greek is more passive — a failure to welcome.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:12 — "the right" vs. "authority" for Greek ἐξουσίαν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he gave to them authority to become children of God" | Literal; "authority" for ἐξουσία |
| LOGOS | "he gave the right to become children of God" | Formal; "right" |
| PNEUMA | "he gave the right to become children of God" | Dynamic; "right" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS/PNEUMA — "the right." In this context ἐξουσία means delegated right/privilege rather than ruling authority.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 1:13 — "bloods" vs. "blood" vs. "human bloodlines" for Greek αἱμάτων (plural)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who not from bloods, nor from [the] will of flesh, nor from [the] will of a man, but from God were born" | Preserves unusual Greek plural "bloods" |
| LOGOS | "who were born, not of blood nor of the will of the flesh nor of the will of man, but of God" | Smooths to singular "blood" |
| PNEUMA | "children born not of human bloodlines, nor of physical desire, nor of any man's decision, but born of God" | Interprets "bloods" as "bloodlines"; "will of flesh" as "physical desire"; "will of man" as "man's decision" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — singular "blood" in English is idiomatic while preserving the meaning. EMET's "bloods" is faithful to the Greek plural but jarring in English. PNEUMA's interpretive expansions ("bloodlines," "physical desire," "man's decision") are helpful commentary but go beyond translation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:14 — "dwelt" vs. "tabernacled" vs. "made his home" for Greek ἐσκήνωσεν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Logos flesh became and tabernacled among us...glory as of [the] only-begotten from [the] Father" | "Tabernacled" — preserves Exodus allusion; retains "Logos" |
| LOGOS | "the Word became flesh and dwelt among us...glory as of the only Son from the Father" | "Dwelt" — standard English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "The Word became a human being and made his home among us...the glory of the Father's one and only Son" | "Made his home" — domesticates the image; "human being" for σάρξ |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "dwelt among us." EMET's "tabernacled" is theologically rich but opaque to most readers. PNEUMA's "made his home" is warm but loses the sacred-space connotation. Also: PNEUMA renders σάρξ as "a human being" rather than "flesh," which softens the shocking physicality of the incarnation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's allusion noted in council notes.

---

## 1:15 — "ranks before me" vs. "has come to be before me" vs. "is far greater than I am" for Greek ἔμπροσθέν μου γέγονεν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The [one] coming after me has come to be before me, for he was first of me." | Literal; "come to be before me" / "first of me" |
| LOGOS | "He who comes after me ranks before me, because he was before me." | Formal; "ranks before me" |
| PNEUMA | "Someone is coming after me who is far greater than I am, because he existed long before me." | Dynamic; "far greater" / "existed long before" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "ranks before me, because he was before me." Clean and captures both temporal and hierarchical priority. PNEUMA's "far greater" and "existed long before" add emphasis not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:16 — "grace upon grace" vs. "one gracious gift after another" for Greek χάριν ἀντὶ χάριτος

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "and grace upon grace" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "grace upon grace" | Formal; identical |
| PNEUMA | "one gracious gift after another" | Dynamic; interprets the idiom |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed EMET/LOGOS — "grace upon grace." The phrase is iconic and should not be paraphrased.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 1:17 — Name forms: "Moses/Jesus Christ" vs. "Mouses/Yeshua Christos"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the law through Mouses was given; the grace and the truth through Yeshua Christos came" | Transliterated names; Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "For the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ." | Anglicized names; English word order |
| PNEUMA | "For the law was given through Moses, but grace and truth came through Jesus Christ." | Adds adversative "but" not in Greek |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's addition of "but" introduces a contrast not explicit in the Greek (though arguably implied). EMET's transliterated names are consistent with his approach but not adopted for consensus.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:18 — "at the Father's side" vs. "in the bosom of the Father" for Greek εἰς τὸν κόλπον τοῦ πατρός

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The only-begotten God, the [one] being in the bosom of the Father — that [one] made [him] known." | Literal; "in the bosom" |
| LOGOS | "the only-begotten God, who is at the Father's side — he has made him known." | Formal; "at the Father's side" |
| PNEUMA | "the one and only God, who is himself God and is at the Father's side — he has revealed him." | Dynamic; "revealed" for ἐξηγήσατο; expands "only-begotten God" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "at the Father's side" for κόλπον. EMET's "bosom" is archaic. PNEUMA's expansion ("who is himself God") is interpretive gloss. "Made him known" preferred over "revealed" as a rendering of ἐξηγήσατο (from which we get "exegesis" — to explain/narrate).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:19 — "the Jews" vs. "the Jewish leaders" for Greek οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Ioudaioi" | Transliterated; avoids interpretive choice |
| LOGOS | "the Jews" | Standard formal translation |
| PNEUMA | "the Jewish leaders in Jerusalem" | Interpretive; adds "leaders" to clarify that this refers to authorities, not the entire people |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "the Jews." PNEUMA's "Jewish leaders" is a reasonable interpretive move (and historically accurate in context) but adds to the text. EMET's transliteration is scholarly but impractical for a reading text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:20 — "confessed" structure and "Christ" vs. "Messiah" for Greek Χριστός

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he confessed and did not deny, and confessed, 'I am not the Christos.'" | Transliterated "Christos" |
| LOGOS | "He confessed, and did not deny, but confessed, 'I am not the Christ.'" | "the Christ" with article |
| PNEUMA | "John stated clearly — he didn't hedge — 'I am not the Messiah.'" | Dynamic paraphrase; "Messiah" instead of "Christ" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "stated clearly — he didn't hedge" is interpretive paraphrase of the triple confession formula. "The Christ" is preferred as the title here (following Sinaiticus context).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:23 — "crying out in the wilderness" vs. "shouting in the wilderness" for Greek βοῶντος

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "voice of [one] crying in the wilderness" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "the voice of one crying out in the wilderness" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "I am a voice shouting in the wilderness, 'Clear the way for the Lord's coming!'" | Dynamic; "shouting" and "Clear the way for the Lord's coming" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "shouting" is more vivid but changes register. More significantly, PNEUMA renders "Make straight the way of the Lord" as "Clear the way for the Lord's coming" — adding "coming" and altering the Isaiah quotation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:26 — "with water" vs. "in water" for Greek ἐν ὕδατι

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I baptize in water" | Literal; ἐν = "in" |
| LOGOS | "I baptize with water" | Formal; instrumental use of ἐν |
| PNEUMA | "I baptize with water" | Dynamic; same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS/PNEUMA — "with water." The instrumental sense of ἐν is standard here, though EMET's "in" is technically literal.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 1:27 — "untie" vs. "loose" for Greek λύσω

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that I should loose the strap of the sandal of him" | Literal; "loose" |
| LOGOS | "the strap of whose sandal I am not worthy to untie" | Formal; "untie" |
| PNEUMA | "I'm not even worthy to be his slave and untie his sandals" | Dynamic; adds "slave" interpretation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "to be his slave and" — an interpretive expansion explaining the cultural significance but not present in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:29 — "Behold" vs. "Look!" for Greek ἴδε

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Behold, the Lamb of the God" | Formal "Behold" with articles |
| LOGOS | "Behold, the Lamb of God" | Formal "Behold" |
| PNEUMA | "Look! The Lamb of God" | Dynamic; modern equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "Behold" is the traditional rendering and suits the solemn declaration.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:32 — "bore witness" vs. "gave this testimony" for Greek ἐμαρτύρησεν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Ioannes testified, saying, 'I have beheld the Spirit descending as a dove out of heaven'" | "beheld"; "as a dove" |
| LOGOS | "John bore witness: 'I saw the Spirit descend from heaven like a dove'" | "bore witness"; "like a dove" |
| PNEUMA | "Then John gave this testimony: 'I saw the Spirit descend from heaven like a dove and rest on him.'" | "gave this testimony"; "rest on" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. Minor variation across all three. EMET's "beheld" is more formal than necessary; PNEUMA's "rest on" anticipates the "remained" later in the verse.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minimal divergence.

---

## 1:34 — "Son of God" — textual variant (ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ vs. ὁ ἐκλεκτὸς τοῦ θεοῦ)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "this is the Son of the God" (notes variant "Chosen One of God") | Follows Sinaiticus; notes variant |
| LOGOS | "this is the Son of God" | Follows Sinaiticus |
| PNEUMA | "this is the Son of God" | Follows Sinaiticus |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three follow Sinaiticus ("Son of God"). EMET's note on the "Chosen One" variant (attested in P5, P106) is preserved in the scholarly apparatus.

**Status:** Consensus. Variant noted for completeness.

---

## 1:38 — "What are you seeking?" vs. "What are you looking for?" for Greek τί ζητεῖτε

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "What do you seek?" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "What are you seeking?" | Formal; progressive aspect |
| PNEUMA | "What are you looking for?" | Dynamic; idiomatic English |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "What are you seeking?" Preserves the deeper resonance of ζητέω (seeking/searching) better than PNEUMA's casual "looking for."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:39 — "the tenth hour" vs. "about four in the afternoon" for Greek ὥρα ἦν ὡς δεκάτη

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The hour was about the tenth." | Literal; preserves ancient time reference |
| LOGOS | "for it was about the tenth hour." | Formal; same ancient reference |
| PNEUMA | "It was about four in the afternoon." | Dynamic; converts to modern time |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "the tenth hour." PNEUMA's conversion to "four in the afternoon" is helpful but assumes Roman time-reckoning (counting from 6 AM). This is debated; some argue John uses a different system.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:41 — "Messiah" vs. "Messias" — transliteration choices

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "We have found the Messias" | Greek transliteration Μεσσίας |
| LOGOS | "We have found the Messiah" (which means Christ) | Anglicized form |
| PNEUMA | "We've found the Messiah!" (which means "Christ") | Same as LOGOS; more exclamatory |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "the Messiah." Standard English form with parenthetical explanation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:42 — "son of John" vs. "son of Ioannes" and "Cephas/Peter" explanation

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "You are Simon the son of Ioannes; you will be called Kephas — which is translated Petros." | Transliterated names throughout |
| LOGOS | "You are Simon the son of John. You shall be called Cephas (which means Peter)." | Anglicized; parenthetical |
| PNEUMA | "You are Simon, the son of John. But you will be called Cephas (which means 'Peter' — 'the Rock')." | Adds "the Rock" interpretation; adds "But" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's addition of "the Rock" is interpretive gloss (accurate but not in the Greek text). PNEUMA's "But" introduces a contrast not explicit in the original.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:46 — "Can anything good come out of Nazareth?" — rendering style

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "From Nazaret can anything good be?" | Literal Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "Can anything good come out of Nazareth?" | Standard English |
| PNEUMA | "'Nazareth!' Nathanael exclaimed. 'Can anything good come from there?'" | Splits into exclamation + question; adds narrative color |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's dramatic splitting is vivid but adds staging directions not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:47 — "an Israelite indeed" vs. "a genuine Israelite" for Greek ἀληθῶς Ἰσραηλίτης

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Behold, truly an Israelite in whom there is no deceit." | Literal; "truly" for ἀληθῶς |
| LOGOS | "Behold, an Israelite indeed, in whom there is no deceit!" | Formal; "indeed" |
| PNEUMA | "Now here is a genuine Israelite — a man without an ounce of deceit." | Dynamic; "genuine"; "without an ounce of deceit" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "an Israelite indeed." PNEUMA's "without an ounce of deceit" is vivid but colloquial, adding a metaphor ("ounce") not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:50 — "do you believe?" question structure

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Because I said to you that I saw you under the fig tree, you believe? Greater [things] than these you will see." | Greek word order; question implied by context |
| LOGOS | "Because I said to you, 'I saw you under the fig tree,' do you believe? You will see greater things than these." | English question form |
| PNEUMA | "You believe just because I told you I saw you under the fig tree? You will see far greater things than this." | Fronts "You believe"; adds "just"; "far greater" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "just" and "far" add emphasis not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:51 — "Truly, truly" vs. "I tell you the solemn truth" for Greek ἀμὴν ἀμήν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Truly, truly I say to you" | Literal double-amen formula |
| LOGOS | "Truly, truly, I say to you" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "I tell you the solemn truth" | Dynamic; captures tone but loses the formal marker |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed EMET/LOGOS — "Truly, truly, I say to you." The Johannine double-amen is a distinctive formal marker appearing 25 times in John. It must be preserved consistently to allow readers to recognize the pattern.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 1:51 — "heaven opened" vs. "the heaven having been opened" — structural difference

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you will see the heaven having been opened, and the angels of the God ascending and descending upon the Son of the Man" | Perfect passive participle; articles throughout |
| LOGOS | "you will see heaven opened, and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man" | Formal; streamlined |
| PNEUMA | "you will see heaven opened and the angels of God going up and coming down on the Son of Man" | "going up and coming down" for ascending/descending |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "going up and coming down" is simpler but loses the register of the angelic vision. "Ascending and descending" is the established English for this Genesis 28 allusion.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| EMET literal vs. LOGOS/PNEUMA idiomatic | 18 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| PNEUMA interpretive expansion (adding words) | 12 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase (rewording) | 10 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Name transliteration (EMET) vs. anglicization | 6 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Word order (Greek vs. English) | 5 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Textual variants | 2 | Consensus (all follow Sinaiticus) |
| LOGOS vs. PNEUMA minor differences | 8 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Chapter 1 reveals the fundamental translation philosophy of each agent. **EMET** consistently preserves Greek word order, articles, transliterated names (Ioannes, Yeshua, Christos), and unusual grammatical features (the plural "bloods," the retained "Logos"). This produces a text that is a mirror of the Greek but often reads awkwardly in English. **LOGOS** produces the most balanced rendering — faithful to the Greek structure but in dignified, readable English. **PNEUMA** prioritizes accessibility, frequently adding interpretive words ("just," "far," "only," "Dear"), converting cultural references to modern equivalents ("four in the afternoon"), and paraphrasing formal constructions into colloquial speech ("he didn't hedge").

SOPHIA's consensus consistently favors **LOGOS** as the baseline, drawing on EMET for scholarly precision (especially in textual notes) and on PNEUMA for meaning insights (especially in commentary). The most theologically significant consensus is on verse 18, where all three translators preserve the Sinaiticus reading "only-begotten God" (μονογενὴς θεός) rather than the later "only-begotten Son."

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
